title: Colored Air
epigraph: Sometimes I go about in pity for myself, and all the while, a great wind carries me across the sky.
epigraphNote: I came across this quote while trying to make sense of the darkness I was in. It stayed with me ever since.
opening: Colored Air was the first name I gave to a change I did not yet understand.

The world itself had not become different, but my relationship to it had. Familiar places began to feel strangely vivid. The ordinary resisted its own familiarity. Children playing in the street, an old man sitting outside his home, flowers returning with the seasons, evening light moving across a familiar wall, nothing extraordinary had happened, yet everything seemed quietly charged with a presence I had spent years overlooking.
The title came before I understood what it meant. It described a feeling more than an idea, as though even the air itself had become saturated with something I had not noticed before.

These photographs were made among the people and places closest to me, my family, my village, the cities I move through, and the ordinary rhythms that shape them. They are not attempts to preserve these moments against time, nor to transform them into symbols. They are traces of encounters, brief moments in which the world quietly revealed itself before returning once again to the ordinary.

sequence.cap: Young girls playing in a neighbourhood in the town of Joun, Lebanon, 2024.
A scene from Sidon during Eid El Fitr celebrations, 2024.
A kid peeking from behind the wall of his house entrance in the town of Joun, Lebanon, 2024.
A Landscape of pine trees at sunset in the town of Joun, Lebanon, 2024.
A candid shot of my little cousin on the balcony of our home in the town of Joun, Lebanon, 2025.
A candid photo of a young boy happily running in one of Sidon’s old streets, 2024.
A portrait of an elderly woman in front of her home in the town of Joun, Lebanon, 2025.
A tree blossoming during spring season in the town of Joun, Lebanon, 2024

fragment: I photograph because the photograph is the most immediate expression of something I have seen and felt. It begins before thought has organized the encounter. The camera does not teach me to look, it follows a way of seeing that is already there.
Color is more than appearance. It carries the inner atmosphere of the moment, not only what the camera recorded, but what being there felt like.

sequence.cap: Kids playing in one of Joun's streets, 2025.
A candid shot of an elderly woman reaching out to hold a child's hand in Sidon city, Lebanon, 2024.

opening: In the village of Joun, the first mulberries of the season are ready.

My father’s uncle Khalil, a quiet man of the land, gardener to others and farmer for himself, asked me to come along. He wanted me to taste the first berries from two trees he had been tending. I often follow him on these small, unhurried walks. There is something about the way he moves through gardens, as though time itself slows down.
I brought my camera with me.

sequence.cap: My father’s uncle, walking down the stairs, leading me to the first mulberry tree, 2025.
My father’s uncle behind the big mulberry tree, picking its fruit, 2025.
My father’s uncle reaching for the mulberry fruit, 2025.
Mulberries resting in the palms of his hands, my father’s uncle showing me the fruit, 2025.
My father’s uncle Khalil holding a mulberry leaf with the fruit nestled in it, as gently as if he were holding newborns, 2025.
My father’s uncle Khalil heading toward the second tree, ready to pick more fruit, 2025.
Khalil reaching up to gather the ripe mulberries, 2025.
Khalil bends toward the ground, where he laid a big blue cloth under the tree to catch the ripe mulberries. Then he carefully picks them up from the cloth, 2025.
The blue cloth laid out under the mulberry tree, filled with the season’s ripe fruit, 2025.
Khalil holding a mulberry leaf with both white and black mulberries on it, 2025.

opening: Photographs from the days I spent with my father’s uncle Anis.
Each evening, he moved quietly among the rose bushes he had tended for years, gathering the flowers one by one as the last light settled over the garden.
I followed with my camera, drawn to the calm in his gestures and to the silence that arrives with the fading light.

sequence.cap: One red rose out at the edge of the garden among the olive trees and wild grass, the hills going pale behind.
Pink roses open all across the bank of bushes, the chain-link fence and the pale hills behind them.
One bud still closed at the end of its stem, standing up into a sky with nothing else in it.
Anis has gone so far into the roses that only his cap, his shoulder and half his face still show.
Anis bent low into the overgrown bushes with his back to me, the roses coming up around him on every side.
My father's uncle standing deep in the canes, his face behind a branch of roses still in bloom, one hand at his side.
Anis moving along the row with his head down, seen through the leaves of the bush in front of me.
Anis stands with his head and shoulders above the row, one hand closed around a bloom, nothing behind him but blue.
My father's uncle Anis close in profile, leaning forward under his pale cap, the bushes in flower behind him.
Anis close to the lens in his pale cap, head lowered, a sprig of roses sharp against the blue beyond him.
Anis's hand comes down through the leaves, the plaid sleeve rolled back, open just above a rose in full bloom.
Anis takes a rose between his fingers at the end of a thorny cane, the light behind him almost white.
Both hands cupped together in front of him, full of the pink roses Anis has just cut.
Anis works his way into the green, the basket of roses hanging from one hand, the other down among the stems.
Anis stops in the gateway with the basket of roses held against his hip, and looks back at me.
The round basket set down among dry seed heads, seen from above, the evening's roses heaped in the middle.
The row of roses at dusk, the flowers going dark in the leaves, a narrow path opening between them.

fragment: A gesture repeated so many times it has become invisible. A door that still opens. The way the light falls in a particular room at a particular hour as if it has been practicing for years.
These are not symbols. They are not evidence of anything. They are just what is here, what keeps being here.

sequence.cap: A candid shot of my father's uncle and my cousin kissing him on the cheek. The town of Joun, Lebanon, 2025.
A photo from a family visit during Eid El Fitr to my father's aunt house in the town of Joun, Lebanon, 2024.

A man receives a haircut from a local barber in the courtyard garden of a modest home at dusk. Rows of potted plants line the perimeter. In the background, two children, distant relatives of the barber, embrace near the entrance, looking toward the camera. The town of Joun, Lebanon, 2025.
A candid photo of two friends holding a flower in the town of Joun, Lebanon, 2024.
A candid photo of friends at sunset in one of Joun's neighbourhoods, 2024.

fragment: There are days the light is more than I can take, and I wait them out in a dark room.

Sorrow does this. It moves through a place the way weather moves through a place, changing what it touches without destroying it. What remains after is not what was there before, but it is not nothing either. It is something else, something that has been through the full weight of what happened and did not empty out.

sequence.cap: Olive trees standing in a sea of wildflowers and grass. The town of Joun, Lebanon 2025.
My uncle's wife holding a bunch of orange blossom in her palms during orange blossom season in the town of Joun, Lebanon, 2024.
Twins in Sidon city, Lebanon, 2024.
A candid shot of a group of kids playing football in Ancient Sidon, Lebanon, 2023.

fragment: Ordinary and ordinary and ordinary until it breaks your heart.

sequence.cap: A photo taken from the rooftop of my home in Joun, Lebanon on a foggy sunset, 2024.

closing: This chapter emerged after a period of personal loss that permanently altered my way of seeing. Yet over time the work moved beyond the event that initiated it. What remained was something simpler, and perhaps more difficult to describe: an increasing attentiveness to the quiet persistence of life itself.
Looking gradually ceased to feel like searching. It became a way of remaining available.
credit: Produced under the guidance of Ali Arkady as part of the Level 1 Visual Journalism Workshop organized by the VII Foundation, 2025.
